title: Capital Structure & Leverage
simple_html:
Capital structure is the mix of debt and equity a company uses to fund itself. Leverage is what happens when you add debt: fixed costs (interest) that magnify both gains and losses. The big question this lesson answers is: is there a best mix of debt and equity that makes a company worth the most?

Why it matters

Debt is cheaper than equity (interest is tax-deductible, and lenders take less risk than shareholders), so a little debt can lower a firm's overall cost of capital and boost returns to shareholders. But too much debt raises the risk of not being able to pay — financial distress or bankruptcy. The art is finding the balance.

Two kinds of leverage

  • Operating leverage — fixed operating costs (rent, salaries). High operating leverage means profits swing a lot when sales change.
  • Financial leverage — fixed financing costs (interest on debt). High financial leverage magnifies earnings per share, up or down.

The key trade-off

The trade-off theory says firms balance the tax benefit of debt (interest reduces taxes) against the costs of financial distress (bankruptcy risk, lost customers, fire-sale assets). The optimal capital structure sits where the next dollar of debt's tax saving just equals its added distress cost.

Key takeaways

  • Capital structure = the debt-versus-equity funding mix.
  • Leverage magnifies returns — and risk — in both directions.
  • Debt adds value through the tax shield but increases bankruptcy risk.
  • There is an optimal mix that minimizes WACC and maximizes firm value.
  • Profitable, stable firms can safely carry more debt than risky, volatile ones.
intermediate_html:
Capital structure is how a firm finances its assets through a combination of debt and equity. Leverage refers to the use of fixed-cost financing (debt) to amplify returns to equity holders. This lesson examines whether the financing mix affects firm value, and if so, what the optimal mix is.

Operating vs. financial leverage

Operating leverage arises from fixed operating costs and is measured by the Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) = % change in EBIT ÷ % change in sales. Financial leverage arises from fixed financing costs and is measured by the Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL) = % change in EPS ÷ % change in EBIT. Their product is the Degree of Total Leverage (DTL), showing how a 1% change in sales flows through to EPS.

The Modigliani–Miller baseline

In a world with no taxes, no bankruptcy costs, and perfect markets, Modigliani and Miller proved that capital structure is irrelevant: firm value depends only on the earning power and risk of its assets, not on how they're financed (MM Proposition I). Their Proposition II adds that the cost of equity rises with leverage, exactly offsetting the cheaper debt — so WACC stays flat.

Introducing taxes and distress

Once we add corporate taxes, debt becomes valuable because interest is tax-deductible. The value of a levered firm = value if unlevered + (Tc × D), the present value of the tax shield. Taken alone, this implies firms should be 100% debt-financed — clearly unrealistic. The missing piece is financial distress costs: direct costs (legal, administrative) and indirect costs (lost sales, defecting suppliers, forced asset sales, management distraction).

Trade-off theory

The trade-off theory states the optimal capital structure balances the marginal tax benefit of debt against the marginal cost of distress. Value rises with debt until distress costs begin to dominate, producing an interior optimum — the debt ratio that maximizes firm value and minimizes WACC.

Pecking-order theory

An alternative view (Myers, 1984) argues that, because of information asymmetry, managers prefer financing in a pecking order: internal funds first, then debt, and equity only as a last resort. This explains why highly profitable firms often use little debt — they don't need external funds.

Worked example: the leverage effect on EPS

A firm has EBIT of $2m. Compare two structures, both financing $10m of assets:

ItemAll-equity50% debt (8%)
EBIT$2.0m$2.0m
Interest (8% × $5m)$0$0.4m
Pre-tax profit$2.0m$1.6m
Tax (25%)$0.5m$0.4m
Net income$1.5m$1.2m
Shares1.0m0.5m
EPS$1.50$2.40

Leverage raises EPS from $1.50 to $2.40 — but if EBIT fell to $0.4m, the levered firm would have zero pre-tax profit while the all-equity firm still earns. Leverage magnifies both directions.

How to apply it

  • Use EBIT–EPS analysis to find the EBIT level above which debt financing beats equity (the indifference point).
  • Set target debt based on cash-flow stability: stable, asset-rich firms can carry more debt; volatile, intangible-heavy firms should carry less.
  • Monitor coverage ratios (interest coverage, debt/EBITDA) to stay clear of distress.
full_html:
Capital structure is the composition of a firm's permanent long-term financing — the proportions of debt, equity, and hybrid securities used to fund its assets. Leverage denotes the use of fixed-cost claims (chiefly debt) that magnify the variability of returns to residual claimants. The central question of capital structure theory is whether, and how, the financing mix affects firm value — a question that connects directly to the firm's WACC (Lesson 6) and ultimately to its objective of maximizing shareholder value (Lesson 1).

Two dimensions of leverage

Operating leverage reflects the proportion of fixed to variable operating costs. The Degree of Operating Leverage, DOL = %ΔEBIT / %ΔSales, captures how sales fluctuations are amplified into operating-profit fluctuations. Financial leverage reflects fixed financing charges; the Degree of Financial Leverage, DFL = %ΔEPS / %ΔEBIT, captures the amplification of EBIT changes into earnings-per-share changes. The Degree of Total Leverage, DTL = DOL × DFL, measures the combined sensitivity of EPS to sales. High total leverage means high upside in booms and severe downside in downturns.

The Modigliani–Miller irrelevance propositions

Modern capital-structure theory begins with Modigliani and Miller (1958). Under perfect markets — no taxes, no transaction or bankruptcy costs, symmetric information, and equal borrowing rates for firms and investors — Proposition I holds that firm value is independent of capital structure: arbitrage ("homemade leverage") ensures that two firms with identical assets but different financing must trade at the same value. Proposition II states Re = Ra + (Ra − Rd)(D/E): the cost of equity rises linearly with leverage, precisely offsetting the substitution of cheaper debt, leaving WACC unchanged. The MM result is powerful not because markets are perfect, but because it tells us where to look for value: in the market imperfections it assumes away.

Taxes and the tax shield

Modigliani and Miller (1963) reintroduced corporate taxes. Because interest is tax-deductible, debt generates a tax shield with present value Tc × D (for perpetual debt). The value of the levered firm becomes VL = VU + Tc·D. This correction implies a counterfactual corner solution — 100% debt — signaling that another offsetting force must exist.

Financial distress and the trade-off theory

That force is financial distress. Direct bankruptcy costs (legal and administrative fees) are modest, but indirect costs — lost customers and suppliers, underinvestment, asset fire-sales, and managerial distraction (Warner, 1977; Andrade & Kaplan, 1998) — can be large. The static trade-off theory (Kraus & Litzenberger, 1973) posits VL = VU + PV(tax shield) − PV(distress costs), yielding an interior optimum where the marginal tax benefit of an extra dollar of debt equals its marginal expected distress cost. At that point WACC is minimized and firm value maximized.

Agency costs

Jensen and Meckling (1976) add agency considerations. Debt disciplines managers by committing free cash flow to interest payments, reducing wasteful empire-building (Jensen's free-cash-flow hypothesis, 1986) — an agency benefit of debt. But debt also creates agency costs: risk-shifting (equityholders favor risky bets when near distress) and the debt-overhang underinvestment problem (Myers, 1977). The optimal structure balances these agency forces alongside taxes and distress.

The pecking-order theory

Myers and Majluf (1984) and Myers (1984) offer an information-asymmetry alternative. Because managers know more than investors, issuing equity signals overvaluation and depresses the share price; firms therefore prefer internal funds, then debt, and issue equity only as a last resort. The pecking order predicts no target debt ratio and explains the empirical regularity that the most profitable firms tend to be the least levered — the opposite of the trade-off theory's prediction. The two theories are complements, not strict rivals; market-timing behavior (Baker & Wurgler, 2002) adds a further layer.

Worked example: EBIT–EPS indifference

Consider financing $10m of assets either all-equity (1.0m shares) or with $5m of 8% debt (0.5m shares), tax rate 25%. At EBIT = $2m, all-equity EPS = $1.5m/1.0m = $1.50, while levered EPS = ($2m − $0.4m)(0.75)/0.5m = $2.40. The indifference EBIT — where both plans give equal EPS — is found by setting the two EPS expressions equal; above it, debt financing yields higher EPS, below it equity does. This frames the risk: leverage helps when EBIT is high and reliable, and hurts when it is low or volatile.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • "Debt is always cheaper, so use more." True for the first marginal dollars, but rising distress and a rising cost of equity eventually push WACC up.
  • Confusing leverage with value creation. Leverage redistributes risk and amplifies returns; it does not change the underlying earning power of assets (the MM insight).
  • Ignoring cash-flow volatility. The right debt level depends on the stability and tangibility of cash flows, not on industry averages alone.
  • Mixing the theories carelessly. Trade-off and pecking-order make different predictions; know which empirical pattern each explains.
  • Book vs. market leverage. Targets and ratios should generally be measured on market values.

Cross-links

Capital structure is inseparable from Cost of Capital & WACC (Lesson 6), which it directly shapes; it builds on Risk, Return & the CAPM (Lesson 5) for the levered cost of equity; and it interacts with Dividend Policy (Lesson 8) through the firm's overall payout-and-financing strategy. All of it serves the value-maximization goal set out in Goals & Scope of Financial Management (Lesson 1).
